name: prefs-baseline
description: User Preferences service. Stores per-user UI/UX preferences with platform-provided keys (language, timezone, density, theme, notification opt-ins) plus per-tenant custom keys that tenants register with their own validation schema. Depends on Identity for user scope. Adopted by Web SDK and Web Runtime.

attributes.summary: Per-user preference store with platform-provided keys + tenant-registered custom keys. Web apps read prefs via the Web SDK; backend services hit the REST API directly.

endpoints.summary: Return the calling user's preference values, merged from platform defaults + tenant defaults + user overrides.

keys.description: Optional comma-separated key filter. Omit to return all keys the user has visibility into.

preferences.description: Map of key → resolved value. Each value carries provenance.

endpoints.summary: Bulk-update the calling user's preferences. Body is a map of key → value; unspecified keys are left unchanged. Returns the new full preference set.
Set a single preference key. Useful for incremental UI updates.
Clear the user's override for a key. The key reverts to tenant default or platform default.
Admin: read another user's preferences. Requires the `admin` role in the user's tenant.
List all registered preference keys (platform-provided + this tenant's custom keys).
Register a custom preference key for this tenant. Requires the `admin` role.

name.description: kebab-case slug, unique within the tenant + platform key set.

endpoints.summary: Unregister a tenant's custom key. Platform-provided keys cannot be deleted. Deleting also clears every user's value for that key.
Set the tenant-default value for a key. Tenant admins use this to override the platform default org-wide.

platform_keys.name: density
platform_keys.description: Maps directly to the Salt SaltProvider density prop.

resolution_order: 2. Tenant default (if the tenant has set one)
3. Platform default (always defined for platform keys)

facts.title: Overview
facts.body: Preferences stores per-user settings with a clean three-layer resolution: user override > tenant default > platform default. Eleven platform-provided keys cover language, timezone, formatting, density, theme, notification opt-ins, and accessibility. Tenants can register custom keys via `POST /v1/preferences/keys` — bring your own validation schema (regex / enum / type) and the service enforces it on writes. Reads always return values with provenance (`source: 'user' | 'tenant_default' | 'platform_default'`) so consumers can show 'set by you' vs 'set by your org' affordances.

facts.title: Registering a tenant-custom preference key
facts.body: Tenants register custom keys via `POST /v1/preferences/keys` (requires `admin` role). The key participates in the same resolution chain as platform keys: user override > tenant default > the `default` you set at registration time. Keys are tenant-scoped — `acme-trading`'s `experimental_features.dark_pool_view` is invisible to `beta-research`. Validation runs on every write: a regex-validated key rejects non-matching values with HTTP 422 and the offending key + message. Deleting a custom key cascades — every user's value for that key is purged in the same transaction.

facts.title: Reading preferences from a web app
facts.body: Use the Web SDK's prefs module — it caches values per session and emits change events when the user updates a setting in another tab. Direct REST calls work too but force the consumer to handle the caching and the user-vs-tenant-default resolution surface. A typical web app reads `density` and `theme` at boot, then subscribes to changes to react to the user toggling a setting elsewhere in the app.

facts.title: Security model
facts.body: All endpoints require Identity-issued bearer tokens. `GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE /v1/preferences/me` are user-scoped — the token's `sub` claim determines the user. `GET /v1/preferences/users/{user_id}` requires `admin` role in the user's tenant. `POST /v1/preferences/keys` and tenant-default endpoints require `admin`. Validation errors return HTTP 422 with field-level violations; auth errors return HTTP 401/403 with no preference data leaked. PII concern: preference values are scanned for emails/phone-numbers on write — flagged values are blocked unless the key's schema explicitly opts into PII (e.g. notification destination addresses).

properties.reason: Every endpoint requires an Identity-issued bearer token. User scope (`/v1/preferences/me`) is derived from the token's `sub` claim.
